reviewed_map.rationale: Pinecone provides full‑text search, embedding, marketplace, and knowledge‑engine capabilities that support evaluation, grounding, and observability of LLM agents.
Both companies focus on open‑source, customizable models and full‑stack deployment options, aligning with YC's emphasis on accessible AI infrastructure.
These providers offer scalable inference, dedicated nodes, and cost‑effective pricing models for production AI workloads.

retrieved_docs.title: YC W25 Request for Startups: AI Infrastructure
retrieved_docs.snippet:
YC W25 Request for Startups: AI Infrastructure

Y Combinator's Winter 2025 Request for Startups highlights AI infrastructure as a top investment thesis. Key areas include: developer tools for agentic AI (debugging, testing, deployment), evaluation frameworks for LLM reliability, multi-modal data pipelines, and cost-optimization layers for large language model inference. YC notes that startups solving agent observability and grounding are especially well-positioned.

retrieved_docs.title: YC Blog — Ycs Newest Visiting Partners
retrieved_docs.snippet:
YC Blog — Ycs Newest Visiting Partners

I'm excited to announce that nine exceptional founders are joining us as Visiting Partners at YC: Matt Riley, Harshita Arora, Grey Baker, Christopher Golda, Raphael Schaad, Christina Gilbert, Francois Chaubard, Vivian Shen, and James Evans.
Visiting Partners are former founders who are taking some time out before their next thing to help advise startups during the batch. They work closely with YC General Partners, sharing hard-won insights from building and scaling their own companies. Each of our new Visiting Partners brings a unique perspective—from design-led products and developer tools to AI infrastructure and edtech.
Matt Riley
Matt was the co-founder and CEO of Swiftype (YC W12), a search-as-a-service company acquired by Elastic in 2017. At Elastic, he was GVP & GM of the Search business unit, and led the product, engineering, design, and developer relations teams that brought Elasticsearch to market. He graduated from the University of Texas at Austin with BS and MS degrees in Electrical Engineering.
Harshita Arora
Harshita Arora grew up in India, where she discovered coding at 13 and dropped out of school at 15 to pursue it full-time. At 16, she built a crypto price tracki

retrieved_docs.title: YC Blog — The Yc Ai Student Starter Pack
retrieved_docs.snippet:
YC Blog — The Yc Ai Student Starter Pack

This year, YC has partnered with nearly two dozen companies to offer students who attend YC events a suite of free credits for AI devtools.
The YC AI Stack includes over $20,000 in cloud credits on Azure and AWS, over $5,000 in credits for GPT/Claude/Grok, and generous credits for AI devtools in voice, browser automation, web search, video generation, databases, web crawling, and more. We’re especially happy that 14 YC companies are represented in this starter pack.
We would love to see students use these to experiment with the latest AI tools without worrying about paying for them out of pocket. Many of the best ideas are built on top of these technologies. For example, you could build a market research tool using Firecrawl and Exa, a voice assistant using Vapi and Browser Use, or a legal aide using Reducto and Gumloop.
At the moment, students who attend a YC university event starting in Fall 2025 are eligible to redeem a deal. You will receive an email shortly after your eligible event confirming your access to the program. We’re excited to work with our partners to continue rolling this out to more students in the upcoming year. Looking forward to seeing what you all build with

retrieved_docs.title: YC Blog — Congratulations To Equipmentshare
retrieved_docs.snippet:
YC Blog — Congratulations To Equipmentshare

When we first met the founders of EquipmentShare in Winter 2015, they didn’t come from Silicon Valley or Wall Street. They came from rural Missouri: the “Show Me” state. Their background is one where you learned to build things yourself, fix what was broken, and work with what you had.
EquipmentShare’s founders grew up in a commune where rules were strict, and self-reliance wasn’t a slogan — it was a necessity. When they left, they didn’t have much. They certainly didn’t have Ivy League degrees. What they did have was something much better: a deep understanding of construction, contractors, and the everyday frustration of trying to get real work done with the wrong tools and the wrong systems.
The EquipmentShare founders didn’t start by trying to “disrupt” an industry. They started by solving their own problem. YC founder Paul Graham proselytized about solving your own problems in his essay about how to get startup ideas: “The most successful startups almost all begin … from things their founders built because there seemed a gap in the world.”
EquipmentShare started as a straightforward idea, which was to level the playing field for contractors by simplifying access to equipment, b

Building the AI Native Cloud
The full stack platform for production AI, powered by cutting-edge systems research.
